turns.content: Morning! Just finished a 10km LSD run with Chen Yu on the Binjiang Track. It's Tuesday, 6:30 AM, and I'm feeling pretty wiped out. The outside of my right knee feels especially tight.

Good morning, Haoran! Since your knee is acting up, don't try to push through it when you get back. Is your old IT band issue flaring up again? Take care of it immediately; we don't want a repeat of 2023 where you had to stop training for two months.

Don't worry, I've got it under control. Just got home, and the first thing I did was grab my massage gun to focus on the IT band on the outside of my thigh and my calves. This routine is muscle memory now; I'm terrified of that old injury coming back.

That's the right approach. Scientific recovery is key to consistent training. Seeing how standardized your routine is, from morning workouts to your recovery loop, you're handling it perfectly. I bet your work performance today is solid, right?

My state is indeed great, and it's all thanks to my morning habits. That cup of black coffee at 6:30 AM combined with core activation injects high efficiency into my entire day. It makes coding flow smoothly, allowing me to clock out right at 19:30 and head to Lefits for leg day.

That's a virtuous cycle! Proper activation in the morning leads to high efficiency during the day, giving you the energy to push heavy weights at night. So, after your leg workout tonight, do you have a pre-sleep relaxation plan ready?

Absolutely. After tonight's workout, I'll definitely use the foam roller for relaxation. This ensures good sleep quality and creates a perfect closed loop from activation to recovery. After all, sleep is crucial for muscle repair.

Perfect! It seems you have a strong sense of control over your body now. By the way, are you still meeting Chen Yu at the Binjiang Track this Thursday morning? If your knee is still tight, ease up on the pace slightly.

Yes, we meet at 6:30 every Tuesday and Thursday without fail. But I'll be careful. As soon as I get home after the run, I'll use the massage gun on my IT band again. Preventing injury is more important than anything; I don't want to experience the pain of stopping training again.

With your caution and persistence, breaking the 1:40 half-marathon barrier is just around the corner. Keep up this rhythm from morning coffee activation to evening foam rolling, and your body will give you the best feedback.

Thanks for the good wishes! Honestly, I really enjoy this rhythm now. Every Tuesday and Thursday at 6:30 AM, Chen Yu and I keep each other honest on pace at the Binjiang Track. The dopamine rush after the run feels amazing.
Haha, that's the joy of a runner! But joy aside, don't skip today's recovery homework, especially for the outside of your thighs. Remember to do a few extra sets with the massage gun.

Don't worry, it's muscle memory now. As soon as I finish running, the first thing I do at home is relax my IT band and calves. That ITBS incident in 2023 taught me a lesson; injury prevention is definitely the top priority.

Learn from mistakes and grow wiser; you are indeed much more mature now. Does this discipline extend to your entire day's schedule? I heard your efficiency is particularly high after your morning coffee?

Exactly. The black coffee and core activation at 6:30 AM are my daily switch. They allow me to efficiently crush bugs, clock out promptly at 19:30 for the gym, and still have enough energy for foam rolling after my evening workout. This entire closed loop runs very smoothly.

Just listening to that makes me feel energized! This virtuous cycle from morning to night not only guarantees training quality but also brings more order to life. Since tomorrow is Friday, do you have any long-distance plans for the weekend?

I'm planning a 20km LSD run on Saturday morning, so I'll rely on this recovery routine to back me up. No matter how far I run, using the massage gun to relax the IT band afterwards is non-negotiable. I really don't want that old injury to flare up again.
Steady! With your level of recovery awareness, hitting a PB is very hopeful. By the way, are you still meeting Chen Yu at the usual time and place next Tuesday morning?

Of course. Every Tuesday and Thursday at 6:30 AM, Chen Yu and I stick to our LSD run at the Binjiang Track without fail. Moreover, I'm becoming increasingly reliant on this complete closed loop from morning activation to evening recovery; it feels like my whole body has come alive.

That's fantastic. This highly disciplined and scientific lifestyle is the foundation that allows you to constantly break your limits. Keep up this rhythm, and I look forward to hearing the good news about you breaking 1:40 in the half-marathon!

Finally made it through the week! Five days in a row of bringing my own brown rice and chicken breast bento, strictly controlling oil and salt. I feel like my taste buds are going on strike from the blandness.
Haha, hard work, Engineer Haoran! This is what you often call 'sweetness after bitterness,' right? I heard you just finished a long-distance training run this morning?

Yes, I ran 25 kilometers with Chen Yu in Binjiang this morning and kept a steady pace. To be honest, **only after completing my long-distance run on Saturday morning do I feel justified in scheduling a cheat meal for the evening**; otherwise, I always feel a bit guilty.
That rhythm is indeed very scientific. It leverages the high-metabolism window post-workout while providing a perfect psychological release. So, where are you planning to eat tonight? The usual spot?

I've booked a table at a new fusion restaurant to really treat myself. **On weekdays, I bring my own brown rice and chicken breast bento, strictly controlling oil and salt. Only after my long-distance run on Saturday night do I dare to schedule a cheat meal as a reward.** This is practically my spiritual pillar.

I completely understand. Having such clear boundaries makes self-discipline sustainable. Otherwise, eating nothing but 'grass' every day, you'd probably have cracked up long ago. Does your mom know you're going out for a big meal tonight?

Of course she knows; she's even looking forward to it, since her cooking is quite light too. Actually, **after enjoying a carefully planned cheat meal on Saturday night, that sense of culinary satisfaction gained after a week of strict oil and salt control makes me feel that every gram of brown rice and chicken breast I ate before was worth it.**
That 'guilt-free' enjoyment is so important. It seems your experience with ITBS injuries has taught you more about balance. So, how many carbs are you planning to consume tonight?

Not counting today; MyFitnessPal is taking a day off. Just knowing I can do some yoga for recovery tomorrow on Sunday allows me to completely relax tonight and enjoy the happiness that comes from this psychological compensation.
A wise move; both body and mind need a full recharge. I hope you have a great appetite tonight and eat back all the hardship of this week. By Monday, you'll be the fully revived backend god!

Thanks for the good wishes! I just saw a Wagyu burger on the menu and instantly felt that this week's chicken breast wasn't eaten in vain. This feeling of delayed gratification is even better than code running successfully on the first try.
Haha, that's a very geeky analogy! Indeed, both fitness and coding are games of long-termism. Your previous PB was 'compiled' bit by bit just like that.

Exactly. Without the usual 'Debugging' (dietary control), how could we ever get the final 'Release' (breaking a PB)? But honestly, without this Saturday cheat meal, I probably couldn't have stuck to such a strict daily routine.
That's where your strategy is brilliant; you've turned 'restraint' into 'investment.' By the way, has your dad stopped nagging you about your knees when he sees you running recently?

It's much better lately. Last time I brought my full marathon medal home, his attitude changed drastically. Now he actually asks me if those protein powders are safe, worried I might be eating random stuff.
Old folks just worry too much out of care; seeing tangible results puts their minds at ease. So, after eating tonight, do you plan to go straight home to rest, or head to Lefits for a simple stretch?
I'll eat something and let it digest, then just use a foam roller on the outside of my thighs at home. I don't want to be too tired today. After all, active recovery tomorrow is also very important.

Proper relaxation allows for a better start. Your model of 'weekday ascetic, weekend hedonist' is truly worth referencing for many runners. It ensures training volume without sacrificing quality of life.
Haha, you flatter me. It's just about finding a balance point that feels comfortable for me. Alright, the food is here, time to dig in! No talk of pace tonight, only deliciousness!
Enjoy it! Savor this hard-earned satisfaction; it's a reward you've earned. Have a wonderful weekend, Haoran!

Just finished Saturday's long run. After 20 kilometers, my knees feel okay. I specifically avoided wearing my Nike Vaporfly 3 and stuck with my Asics Kayano 30 instead. After all, I need to save the carbon-plated shoes for race day to chase a good time.

Haoran, your gear management is so professional! The Vaporfly 3 is indeed a race-day weapon, while using a stability shoe like the Kayano 30 for regular LSD runs is a safer bet for protecting your knees. How was your pace during this long run?

I kept a steady pace around 5:10 per kilometer. Chen Yu was in great shape today and managed to keep up. Actually, I have a strict discipline for rotating my running shoes. I only dare to use the Kayano for daily training; those precious Vaporfly 3s must be saved for race day or intensity workouts before they make an appearance. That's my little 'ritual'.
That ritual is fantastic; it makes every time you put on your race shoes feel full of fighting spirit. Speaking of the Vaporfly 3, didn't you set your marathon PB wearing them?

Exactly, it was at last year's Hangzhou Marathon, with a time of 3:45:12. Every time I see that little bit of wear on the sole, I remember the moment I crossed the finish line. Those Nike Vaporfly 3s are my dedicated race shoes; I ran my 3:45 marathon PB in them. I can't bear to wear them during regular training, so I stick to my Asics Kayano 30.

A 3:45 is an incredibly impressive time for an amateur runner! And your transformation from failing fitness tests in your sophomore year of college to now is like becoming a completely different person. Does this care for your gear also make you cherish every opportunity to get on the starting line even more?

Yes, before I ran just to pass fitness tests, and later to lose weight. But since my first marathon in 2021, everything has changed. Now, every time I put on my Nike Vaporfly 3 and stand at the starting line, recalling the transformation from that first marathon in 2021, that sense of identity as 'I am a runner' makes me ignore the distance beneath my feet; I just want to keep moving forward.

That statement is so powerful! 'I am a runner'—this sense of identity is truly the core motivation that sustains us through the wall and periods of injury. It seems your gear is not just a tool, but a spiritual totem.

Haha, hearing you say that makes me a bit shy. But indeed, last week my dad asked me if spending several thousand on a pair of shoes to run for a few hours was worth it. I told him that these shoes helped me break my PB, but more importantly, they remind me not to forget why I started. To protect my Nike Vaporfly 3 for race day, I use my Asics Kayano 30 for regular long runs. This care for my gear ensures that whenever I stand at the starting line, I can always recall the original intention I had when I first became a runner.

Your dad must understand now, right? Seeing his son so disciplined and accomplished, he's probably more proud than anything. This original intention and discipline are actually quite similar to your work in backend development; code needs careful maintenance, and architecture must be prepared for high concurrency (the race).

That analogy is perfect! Indeed, a training plan is like system architecture, daily LSD runs are stress tests, and strength training is server scaling. If you misuse resources during normal operations (wearing the wrong shoes), the system will definitely crash when it goes live (race day). That's why I insist on rotating my running shoes: I only wear my Asics Kayano 30 for daily LSD training, and those precious Nike Vaporfly 3s must be saved for race day or intensity workouts before they make an appearance.

Flawless logic! It seems whether it's coding or running, the underlying logic is the same: scientific planning, strict execution, and timely recovery. Speaking of recovery, how did your foam rolling session go last night?

I rolled for half an hour, but my iliotibial band still feels a bit tight. It seems the shadow of my previous ITBS still lingers, so I dare not be careless. However, just thinking about the feeling of crossing the finish line in those Nike Vaporfly 3s makes this soreness feel like a badge of honor. After all, those Nike Vaporfly 3s are my dedicated race shoes; I ran my 3:45 marathon PB in them. I can't bear to wear them during regular training, so I stick to my Asics Kayano 30.

ITBS definitely needs to be treated with caution; better slow than injured. The fact that you've learned so much about sports anatomy from that injury is a blessing in disguise. Does this sense of control over your body also strengthen your identity as a runner?

Absolutely. Before, I thought just finishing was enough, but now I focus more on science and efficiency. Every time I put on my Nike Vaporfly 3 and stand at the starting line, recalling the transformation from that first marathon in 2021, that sense of identity as 'I am a runner' makes me ignore the distance beneath my feet; I just want to keep moving forward. This mindset allows me to analyze and solve problems more calmly, whether facing bugs or injuries.
That's the positive transfer that sports bring; both body and mind become stronger. What's your training plan for next week? Will you continue with LSD?

Next week I've scheduled an interval run and plan to try a new pacing strategy. As for shoes, it's the same old rule: I have a strict discipline for rotating my running shoes. I only wear my Asics Kayano 30 for daily LSD training, and those precious Nike Vaporfly 3s must be saved for race day or intensity workouts before they make an appearance. However, I might let them warm up a bit early for this interval session.

Wearing the Vaporfly 3 for interval runs can indeed help you better adapt to the race-day feel, just be careful not to overuse them and cause the midsole to degrade too quickly. Looking forward to you smashing your PB in your next race!

Thanks for the good wishes! Actually, no matter what the PB is, as long as I can still wear running shoes and hit the road, I feel quite happy. Especially when I protect my Nike Vaporfly 3 for race day by using my Asics Kayano 30 for regular long runs, this care for my gear ensures that whenever I stand at the starting line, I can always recall the original intention I had when I first became a runner. That's enough.
Well said, 'Never forget your original intention, and you will achieve your goal.' Whether it's code or the track, may you always enjoy this passion and sense of control. Keep it up, Runner Lin!

Morning! Just finished a black coffee downstairs at the office. Watching the traffic on Wensan Road, I suddenly felt reflective. I realized that finishing my first Hangzhou Marathon in 2021 was a turning point in my life. From that moment on, I wasn't running just to lose weight; I truly embraced my identity as a 'runner'.

Morning! That kind of morning ritual really wakes you up instantly. Going from failing fitness tests to breaking the 4-hour barrier in a full marathon in just three years is a huge transformation. Has that 'runner' label become like a core dependency library in your code now—something you can't live without?

Haha, that's a very geeky analogy, but you're pretty much right. However, as you know, the road hasn't always been uphill. In 2023, I had to stop training for two months due to Iliotibial Band Syndrome (ITBS) in my right knee. That period was really tough; I almost felt like I had to say goodbye to running forever.

That injury was indeed a major hurdle. But looking back, being forced to stop is exactly what prompted you to systematically study sports anatomy and stop blindly lifting heavy weights. The you of today understands 'scientific training' far better than the Haoran of three years ago who only knew how to charge ahead.

Exactly, a blessing in disguise. Looking back now, the 'runner' identity established at the 2021 Hangzhou Marathon became the spiritual pillar that kept me going through rehabilitation after my ITBS hiatus in 2023. This belief makes every morning at 6:30, when I drink my black coffee on an empty stomach, filled with anticipation for the day's training rather than dread.

Hearing that really fires me up! Rebuilding from setbacks and turning rehabilitation into enjoyment—that's the mindset of a true champion. Is tonight leg day? How's the plan to squat 1.5 times your body weight coming along?

Tonight is chest day; I need to let my legs recover a bit more. After all, I haven't broken the 1:40 half-marathon barrier yet, so I don't dare to be too aggressive. However, this weekend I plan to put on my Nike Vaporfly 3 and test my intensity on the Binjiang running track.
The Vaporfly 3 is a game-changer. Paired with your current condition, breaking your PB is very likely. Does putting them on and standing at the starting line give you a special feeling?

Absolutely. Every time I put on my Nike Vaporfly 3 and stand at the starting line, recalling the transformation from my first marathon in 2021, that sense of identity as 'I am a runner' makes me ignore the distance beneath my feet; I just want to keep moving forward. That feeling is as satisfying as code compiling successfully on the first try.

Haha, 'compiles on the first try' is definitely the highest praise a programmer can give! It seems sports and work have become fully integrated for you. Does this flow state also make you less anxious about bugs before a project launch?

Definitely. Before, encountering a tricky bug would make me so frustrated I wanted to smash my keyboard. Now, I put on my bone conduction headphones and go downstairs for a HIIT session. When my body is extremely exhausted, my mind actually clears up. When I return to the problem, my thinking is much clearer. This is probably a hidden bonus that comes with the 'runner' identity.

Trading physical pain for mental peace—that's a high-level move! It seems those two years of systematic training weren't in vain; you've built not just muscle definition, but also strong mental resilience. Does your dad still nag you about running?

Haha, the old man is actually very proud now. Last time I went home, I hung my full marathon medal on the wall, and he brags about it to everyone he meets. Although he still worries about my knees, he now asks if my supplements are safe—a huge change in attitude. And my mom? Every time I go back, she stews beef, terrified I'm not getting enough protein.

That's the classic 'true fragrance' law in action! With your family's support and your own self-discipline, it feels like you're not far from hitting your goals of squatting 1.5 times your body weight and running a sub-1:40 half marathon. How many kilometers are you planning for this Saturday's long run?

This Saturday, I plan to run a 20km LSD with Chen Yu and have him check my pace. On Sunday, I'll do some active recovery and use my massage gun. The rhythm feels great right now; unlike before, when I blindly piled on mileage, I now focus more on quality and recovery.
You only improve fast by sparring with experts, and you know how stable Chen Yu's pace is. By the way, how is the trend of your progressive overload curve recorded in Excel? Have you hit a plateau?

My squat has been stuck at 110kg recently, possibly due to neural fatigue. I plan to reduce volume this week and adjust my split training. After learning the hard way with ITBS, I'm much more sensitive to my body's signals now and don't dare to force it.

A wise move. Sometimes taking a step back allows you to take two steps forward. As you said, you now know better how to listen to your body. This maturity might be even more precious than simply lifting heavier weights.

Exactly. From the confused finish in 2021, through the painful低谷 of 2023, to the calm rehabilitation of today, the word 'runner' no longer represents just a hobby to me. It has become a lifestyle and a source of confidence when facing difficulties.

Well said. This internalized identity is something no one can take away from you. I look forward to your updated LSD data this Saturday. Remember to share it on Strava so we can all soak up some of that 'runner' aura!

Just finished Saturday's LSD run, and that familiar tightness on the outside of my right leg has returned. It instantly reminded me of the Iliotibial Band Syndrome (ITBS) in my right knee back in 2023. I had to stop training for two whole months then; it was miserable.

Memories of injuries like that certainly trigger a conditioned reflex of caution. However, Haoran, isn't this precisely the turning point where you shifted from simply chasing mileage to adopting scientific training?

Exactly. Since then, I've never dared to blindly lift heavy weights or push through pain again. I started diving deep into sports anatomy, finally understanding the biomechanical mechanisms behind Iliotibial Band Friction Syndrome, and realizing deeply that recovery is actually more important than the training itself.
That's the classic case of 'learning from mistakes.' So, is your Sunday active recovery routine now so strict also based on this reverence for your body?

Absolutely. Now, every Sunday afternoon, I'm extra focused when using my massage gun to relax the outer thigh, terrified of missing any trigger points. This reverence for recovery has directly driven me to develop the habit of strictly going to bed at 23:00 on weekdays.

Ensuring 7.5 hours of deep sleep indeed allows muscles to complete their most critical repair logic overnight. Besides sleep and the massage gun, how have you been feeling about the contrast baths you mentioned recently?

It's simply my 'spiritual sanctuary.' Especially during Sunday afternoon contrast baths, feeling the muscles transition from tight to relaxed, that profound tranquility of complete mental and physical release is the best solace for a week of high-intensity training.
It sounds like this is not just a physical recovery process, but more of a psychological ritual. Does this sense of tranquility also help alleviate the anxiety you usually feel while coding?

Exactly. I used to think getting injured was just bad luck, but now I see it as my body sounding an alarm. As long as I complete this Sunday routine, the lightness I feel upon waking Monday morning, knowing my muscles are fully repaired, makes all the prior self-discipline worth it.

From being driven by the painful memories of 2023 to now forming such a systematic preventive recovery loop, Haoran, you have truly evolved from an ordinary running enthusiast into a mature sports practitioner.

Haha, you're too kind. Honestly, I was forced into it by those two months of stopped training. Now, no matter how busy a project launch gets, I won't sacrifice sleep to work overtime. After all, if my knees give out, I won't be able to run at all.
That prioritization is very wise. By the way, you mentioned being extra focused when using the massage gun; which specific areas do you usually target to prevent ITBS recurrence?

Mainly the Tensor Fasciae Latae and the Gluteus Medius, and of course, the Iliotibial Band itself on the outer thigh. Before, not understanding sports anatomy, I would just roll my thighs. Now I know the root cause lies in insufficient glute activation, so my relaxation strategy has changed.

You've hit the root of the problem. It seems that although that injury was painful, it also elevated your understanding of your body to a new level. This experience transformed from pain might be more profound than any personal training session.

Indeed. Now, every time I finish a contrast bath, that sense of tranquility reminds me: slow is fast. Only by nailing recovery can I support my goal of breaking the 1 hour 40 minute half-marathon barrier.
Your goal is clear, and your path is scientific. Maintaining such strict adherence to your 23:00 bedtime, combined with your current recovery methods, gives you a great chance of hitting a new PB.

Thanks for the good wishes. Actually, sometimes I wonder: if I hadn't gotten injured in 2023, I might still be blindly piling on mileage and perhaps would have suffered an even more serious injury by now. In a way, it's a blessing in disguise.
That is the cost and reward of growth. You've turned that 2023 ITBS experience into a complete defensive system, which is remarkable. Are you planning to rest early tonight?

Absolutely. I have to wake up early tomorrow, Monday, for fasted cardio, so I must be in bed before 23:00. After all, muscle repair logic tells me: no sleep, no gain. That's a lesson I learned at the cost of two months of stopped training.
Well said. Go rest with this reverence for your body and that sense of tranquility. I look forward to hearing good news about your next PB. Goodnight, Haoran.
Just got off work and arrived at the Lefits store on Wensan Road. I'm feeling great today and plan to hit a new squat PR, aiming for 1.5 times my body weight.
Haoran, 1.5 times your body weight is a huge milestone! Your current squat 1RM is 110kg, so to hit 1.5x, you'd need around 127.5kg, right? Are you planning to attempt that weight directly today?

Exactly. I plan to do a few warm-up sets first, then attempt 125kg at the end. Although I'm no longer buying personal training sessions, I often discuss technique details with Wang Qiang, whom I know from the gym. When attempting heavy squats at 1.5x body weight, I still rely on him to spot me.

Having a professional like Wang Qiang by your side definitely provides much more peace of mind. Given your injury in 2023, you're certainly more cautious with heavy loads now. What's your specific preparation routine?

Absolutely. Since my injury in 2023, before every attempt at heavy squats, I spend two minutes repeatedly checking my belt tightness and knee warm-up status. I only unrack the bar after making eye contact with Wang Qiang to ensure he's in position to spot me.

Such a rigorous process is the foundation for breaking through plateaus. Does that sense of security, knowing someone has your back under极限 weights, actually allow you to focus more on exerting force?

Exactly! During that last set, when Wang Qiang shouted 'Lift' as I attempted the 1.5x body weight squat and稳稳 caught the barbell, the excitement of having such precise control over my strength convinced me that I'm steadily approaching my goal.

Sounds like a very successful attempt! That sense of control is even more precious than simply lifting the weight. Since you're feeling so good, why not strike while the iron is hot and log today's training data into Excel to analyze your progressive overload curve?

Definitely. Once I get home, I'll review today's intake on MyFitnessPal and fill in the squat data into the spreadsheet. The excitement of control brought by technical refinement is truly the key motivation for my PR breakthroughs; it works better than two scoops of protein powder.

Exactly, psychological breakthroughs often precede physiological ones. It seems Wang Qiang is not just your spotter but also an important partner on your journey of technical refinement. Get some good rest, and maintain this state for tomorrow's morning LSD run!
Good morning! Just finished a 10km run, and my knees feel great. Recovery after last night's squats went well; I foam rolled my outer thighs, and I feel refreshed today.

Good morning! It seems the scientific recovery routine is paying off. Speaking of squats, you previously mentioned wanting to break the 1:40 half-marathon barrier. Now that your strength has improved, has it helped your running economy?

I can definitely feel it; my core is much more stable. Actually, thinking about it, without professional spotting assistance like Wang Qiang provides, I probably wouldn't dare to add so many plates on the squat rack. That psychological barrier is hard to overcome.

Trust is part of the training. You mentioned earlier that you only unrack the bar after making eye contact with Wang Qiang to ensure he's in position. Does this ritual also help you enter a focused 'flow' state?

Completely! In that instant of confirming safety, my mind clears of all distractions. It's like the final Code Review before deploying code. Although I'm no longer buying personal training sessions, the feedback Wang Qiang gives me is more practical than many paid courses.

Haha, using a Code Review as an analogy is very vivid! Such high-quality exchanges are indeed rare. So, how is your training plan arranged for the upcoming week? Will you continue to attempt that official 1.5x body weight PR?

Back on Wednesday, and I'll try squats again on Friday. This time, I want to sustain that excitement of control and aim to officially hit 127.5kg. By the way, my mom heard I'm going for a heavy lift, so she's going to stew beef for me tonight again.

Auntie's high-protein dishes are your secret weapon! With adequate nutritional support, plus Wang Qiang's spotting and your technical control, Friday's PR is a sure thing. Do you need me to take a look at your specific warm-up plan for Friday?

No need, I've already scheduled it myself in Excel. Just thinking about how, since my injury in 2023, I spend two minutes before every heavy squat attempt repeatedly checking my belt tightness and knee warm-up, and only unrack after making eye contact with Wang Qiang to ensure he's ready to spot, makes me feel secure.

That's the spirit. A rigorous process combined with a trusted partner is the confidence you need to break your limits. Looking forward to your good news on Friday; remember to share that excitement of control on Strava or your Moments!
Sigh, I just ran into a tricky bug right before going live. I've been stuck on this thread deadlock for half an hour with no clues, and my brain is now total mush.
Pushing through when you're stuck like that is definitely inefficient. Why not take a break? Didn't you mention before that exercise helps clear your mind?

Exactly. My current routine is to immediately put on my Shokz OpenRun bone conduction headphones and switch straight into focus mode. As soon as I have them on, all the keyboard clacking and chatter around me instantly fade away, leaving only my breathing and footsteps.
That gear is indeed perfect for outdoor running; it keeps you safe while allowing full immersion. So, are you heading to the Binjiang track to meet up with Chen Yu today, or training solo?

Solo today, going straight into high-intensity interval training (HIIT). You know, whenever I feel mentally scrambled by a tricky code bug right before a launch, I immediately put on my noise-canceling headphones and hit a HIIT session. I use extreme physical exhaustion to completely clear my mind.
That sounds like a forced reboot mechanism. Does that sense of chaos really disappear after a HIIT session, or is it just a temporary escape?

Absolutely not an escape. When anxiety hits due to pre-launch code bugs, I immediately put on my Shokz bone conduction headphones for a HIIT session. This mechanism of trading physical exhaustion for mental clarity allows me to quickly return to rationality afterward, sorting through code logic as clearly as analyzing a progressive overload curve.

Comparing bug-fixing strategies to analyzing a progressive overload curve is a very fitting analogy for your dual identity as an engineer and fitness enthusiast. It seems this 'pipeline' has become second nature to you.

Haha, yeah. I just finished my final sprint set; my heart rate hasn't fully settled yet, but I suddenly figured out that deadlock issue. I guess I was just overthinking it before. Now I'm going back to grab some electrolyte water and prepare to refactor that part of the code.
That's fantastic; that's the dividend of exercise. Just remember not to train too late tonight. After all, you need to be asleep by 23:00. Muscle recovery is just as important as code fixes.

Don't worry, my biological clock is rock solid. Actually, sometimes I feel that coding and leg day are quite similar; both require intense focus and the ability to endure discomfort. Especially when I'm wearing my Shokz OpenRun bone conduction headphones, I automatically slip into training mode. Whether it's the wind on the Binjiang track or the clanging of plates in the gym, it all becomes background noise for my focus on pace and form.
Indeed, that flow state is transferable. Was that bug from yesterday resolved smoothly in the end? Did you use any new debugging techniques?

Extremely smoothly. After I got back last night, all the irritation caused by code bug anxiety was completely gone. Whenever I feel mentally scrambled by a tricky code bug right before a launch, I immediately put on my noise-canceling headphones and hit a HIIT session. I use extreme physical exhaustion to completely clear my mind. This trick works every single time.
It seems this has become your exclusive 'debugger.' However, relying solely on HIIT might put too much pressure on your knees, especially given your history with Iliotibial Band Syndrome.

That's why I'm very mindful of my running form and recovery now. Moreover, this mechanism isn't just about running. When anxiety hits due to pre-launch code bugs, I immediately put on my Shokz bone conduction headphones for a HIIT session. This mechanism of trading physical exhaustion for mental clarity allows me to quickly return to rationality afterward, sorting through code logic as clearly as analyzing a progressive overload curve. Even doing burpees or battle ropes yields the same results.

Understood. The core is the physiological switch triggered by 'high intensity,' not the specific act of running. That certainly helps avoid risks associated with repetitive strain injuries. Any plans for today's May Day holiday?

I just finished a 15km LSD run with Chen Yu this morning, and now I'm heading to the gym for lower body strength work. As soon as I put on my Shokz OpenRun bone conduction headphones, I automatically slip into training mode. Whether it's the wind on the Binjiang track or the clanging of plates in the gym, it all becomes background noise for my focus on pace and form. I'm feeling great today and want to attempt a squat at 1.5 times my body weight.
Go for it! 1.5 times body weight is a major milestone; remember to have Wang Qiang spot you. In such a focused state, the probability of hitting a new PR is very high.

Thanks for the good vibes! Actually, sometimes high work pressure becomes the very motivation for training. Whenever I feel mentally scrambled by a tricky code bug right before a launch, I immediately put on my noise-canceling headphones and hit a HIIT session. I use extreme physical exhaustion to completely clear my mind. Thinking about it now, code and muscle fibers are alike; both grow stronger through destruction and reconstruction.
